title: Polymer clay marble-effect coasters
subtitle: Two-colour marbled sheets baked flat and cut into coaster rounds
excerpt: Four coasters cut from a marbled polymer clay sheet and baked in a domestic oven. The marbling comes from folding two contrasting colours together and rolling them out before they fully blend. Decorative use; these are not food-safe and not suitable for hot beverages.
sourceNotes: Polymer clay marbling technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: PVC-based modelling material that sets in a domestic oven at 110-130°C. Decorative use only.
The heat process that sets polymer clay in a domestic oven at 110-130°C.

content.text: Marble-effect polymer clay coasters are built from two contrasting
polymer clay

colours twisted together and rolled flat before they fully combine. The trick is to stop rolling while the colours are still visibly separate; that suspended state is the marble pattern. Over-blending produces a flat, single-colour sheet. Decorative only: these coasters are not food-safe and are not intended for hot cups, which can soften baked polymer clay.

attrs.title: Baking polymer clay
attrs.body: Use a dedicated oven liner or tray. Verify the oven temperature with a thermometer. Do not share the baking surface with food cookware.

items.name: Polymer clay, two contrasting colours
items.substitution: White and black, or white and terracotta, produce a classic marble look

items.name: Round cutter, 9 cm diameter
items.substitution: Trace around a cup of that diameter with a craft knife

content.text: Making the coasters
1. Condition both colours
Condition each colour separately by working in the hands for two to three minutes until fully pliable. Roll each into a log about 15 cm long.
2. Twist and fold

Lay the two logs side by side and twist them together three or four times. Fold the twisted log in half, twist twice more, and fold again. At this point the colours are interlocked but still visible as distinct strands. Do not over-work; five or six twists total is the upper limit before the colours muddy.
3. Roll to a flat sheet

Press the twisted mass flat between two sheets of greaseproof paper and roll to 4 mm thickness. The stretching and flattening motion draws the two colours into veins rather than layers. Each coaster will look different depending on how the marble pattern falls.
4. Cut the coasters

Cut four 9 cm circles from the sheet. Position each cutter over the most interesting area of the marble pattern. Gather the trimmings and fold them together for a further, more fully blended marble coaster, or store for another project.
5. Bake
Place the cut circles on the baking tray, flat. Bake at 110°C (fan) for 25-30 minutes.
Curing
polymer clay at 4 mm thickness takes longer than thinner pieces; check at 25 minutes by pressing a fingernail lightly into the cool edge. A fully cured piece does not dent.
Finishing

Once cool, sand the edges with 400-grit sandpaper to remove any irregular cuts. Apply a light coat of polymer clay glaze or clear acrylic varnish if a polished surface is wanted. Stick a 9 cm circle of cork or felt to the base of each coaster to protect surfaces.
Care

Polymer clay coasters are for cool and room-temperature use. Hot mugs can soften and distort baked polymer clay, and the material is not food-safe. Wipe clean with a damp cloth; do not submerge in water.

items.symptom: Colours fully merged, no marble pattern visible
items.fix: The fully merged sheet is a usable solid colour; start again with fresh clay and stop at four or five twists

items.cause: Baking tray was not flat, or coasters slid during preheating
items.fix: Place a flat ceramic tile on top of the coasters while baking to weight them flat
